Facts.text: Friezen robbers murder Bishop Boniface (later Saint) and over 50 companions near Dokkum
Kraków, Poland, receives city rights
Battle of Worringen: Jan I, Duke of Brabant defeats army of Archbishop Siegfried II of Cologne, one of the largest and fiercest battles of the Middle Ages
Archbishop Bertrand the Got of Bordeaux elected Pope Clement V
England and Netherlands sign trade agreement
Spanish troops under Spinola conquer Breda
Prince William of Orangebecomes Knight of Garter
US Congress passes the Neutrality Act, banning Americans from serving in foreign armed forces
The Battle of New Ross: The attempt to spread United Irish Rebellion into Munster is defeated
First recorded tornado in "Tornado Alley" (Southern Illinois)
1st trotter to break 3 minute mile (Yankee)
Batavian Republic becomes Kingdom of Holland
Turks capture the Acropolis and take Athens during the Greek War of Independence
HMS Pickle captures the armed slave ship Voladora off the coast of Cuba
Anti-monarchist forces launch an uprising in Paris, starting the unsuccessful June Rebellion
Telegraph line opens between Philadelphia and Baltimore
Danish National Day, Denmark becomes a constitutional monarchy
Anti-foreign anti-Roman Catholic Know-Nothing Party's 1st convention
Walter Woodbury and James Page open photo studio in Batavia (Jakarta)
Federal marshals seize arms and gunpowder at the DuPont Works in Delaware
Battle of Franklin's Crossing, Virginia (Deep Run)
CSS Alabama captures "Talisman" in the Mid Atlantic
Battle of Piedmont, Virginia (Augusta City)
French colonial expedition to explore Mekong River (world's 12th longest, though Southeast Asia) sets out from Saigon, led by Ernest Doudart de Lagrée
Great Fire of Pera in a district of Constantinople, the wealthiest part of the city, kills hundreds
Republican National Convention meets in Philadelphia
Sultan Barghash bin Said, under British pressure, closes the infamous slave market of Zanzibar in present-day Tanzania
Pacific Stock Exchange formally opens in San Francisco
Bananas become popular in the US at the Centennial Exposition in Philadelphia
American outlaw John Wesley Hardin convicted of the murder of Deputy Sheriff Charles Webb, sentenced to 25 years in prison
Storm & floods hits Bombay; about 100,000 die
Unusual Rio de la Plata Earthquake measures magnitude 5.5
Pretoria, capital of the Boer Republic of South Africa, falls to the British led by General Buller
Determined to keep pace with Britain as a major naval power, the German Reichstag passes new navy legislation, increasing the total tonnage in Germany's fleet
Red Sox Joe Wood strikes out 3 pinch hitters in 9th for 5-4 win
US marines invade Caimanera, Cuba
Dutch Disability laws go into effect
Denmark amends its constitution to allow women's suffrage
Louis Brandeis (59) is sworn in as an Associate Justice of the Supreme Court of the United States; he serves until 1939
The Sherif Hussein proclaims a revolt of the Arabs in the province of Hejaz, an action that undermines the Turkish Empire
10 million US men begin registering for draft in WW I
Philadelphia Athletics's VP Thomas Shibe denies charges that baseballs are livelier
The Banker's committee of the Reparations Commission refuses an international loan to Germany
Clevelands Indians baseball team triple-play NY Yankees & win 15-3
Ramsey MacDonald forms minority Labour government in Britain
Jules Renkin (68) becomes Prime Minister of Belgium
US drops the Gold Standard when Congress enacts a joint resolution nullifying creditors right to demand payment in gold
1st formal meeting of Baker Street Irregulars (NYC)
A synthetic rubber tire is exhibited in Akron, Ohio, by Goodyear Tire & Rubber Company
American Negro Theater organizes
General Von Bock starts a German offensive in the Somme
Governor of Suriname and the Netherlands Antilles refuse entry to Jewish refugees
Netherlands rations petroleum
WWII: Battle of France - Fall Rot (Case Red), Germany invaded northern France, crossing the River Somme
German ammunition depot at medieval Smederovo Fortress, near Belgrade, Serbia explodes; kills close to 2,000
World War II: At least 4,000 people who hid in a tunnel die after a Japanese air attack on the Chinese city of Chongqing
An explosion at the Joliet Army Ammunition Plant kills 48 people
British offensive in North Africa under General Ritchie
USA declares war on Bulgaria, Hungary & Romania
German occupiers arrest Louvain University's chancellor
Philippine President José P. Laurel survives assassination attempt after being shot 4 times with a 45 caliber pistol while playing golf at Wack Wack Golf Course in Mandaluyong
1st B-29 bombing raid; 1 plane lost due to engine failure
Allied forces march into Rome
As part of Operation Tonga, the 1st British gliders touch down on French soil to prepare for the D-Day invasion
WWII: Allied Powers (US, UK, USSR, and France) assume supreme authority over Germany with the "Berlin Declaration Regarding the Defeat of Germany"
Dutch comic strip "Eric de Noorman" by Hans G. Kresse is launched
Fire at LaSalle Hotel cocktail lounge kills 61 in Chicago, Illinois
"(Ghost) Riders in the Sky: A Cowboy Legend" single released by Stan Jones
US Supreme Court undermines legal foundations of segregation
Test Cricket debut of Freddie Trueman v India at Headingley
Denmark adopts a new constitution
US Senate rejects China People's Republic membership to UN
"Your Show Of Shows" last airs on NBC-TV
US Federal court rules racial segregation on Montgomery buses anti-Constitutional
NY narcotics investigator, Dr Herbert Berger, urges AMA to investigate use of stimulating drugs by athletes
Bob Dylan graduates from Hibbing High School in Minnesota
"The George Gobel Show" last airs on CBS-TV
British Minister of War John Profumo resigns due to Christine Keeler scandal
Dutch Princess Marijke changes her name to Christina
Rolling Stones 1st US concert tour (with Bobby Goldsboro & Bobby Vee) debuts in San Bernadino, California
"Wooly Bully" by Sam the Sham & Pharaohs hits #2
Lopez Arellano becomes president of Honduras
Cincinnati Red Leo Cardenas hits 4 HRs in a doubleheader

Judge in Peoria, Illinois sentences American mass murderer Richard Speck to death in electric chair, after his jury conviction; later reduced to minimum 100 years in prison, he dies of a heart attack in prison in 1991
Royal Canadian Mint is ordered to start converting 10-cent and 25-cent coins to pure nickel as soon as possible
WSBE-TV channel 36 in Providence, Rhode Island (PBS) begins broadcasting
Dutch Antilles government of Kroon resigns
International communist conference begins in Moscow
Race riot in Hartford, Connecticut
Chile becomes a member of the Berne Convention copyright treaty
KPAX TV channel 8 in Missoula, Montana (CBS) begins broadcasting
The Falls Road curfew in North Ireland, imposed by the British Army while searching for IRA weapons, is lifted after a march by women breaches the British Army cordon
"If You Had Wings" ride opens at Walt Disney World
UN Conference on Human Environment opens in Stockholm
48th US National Spelling Bee: Hugh Tosteson wins spelling incisor
The California Agricultural Labor Relations Act (CALRA), which establishes collective bargaining for farmworkers, becomes law
United Kingdom electorate votes 67% to 33% in a referendum to remain part of the European Common Market
"Bigfoot" by Bro Smith hits #57
After a suspected republican bombing kills 2 Protestant civilians in a pub, the Ulster Volunteer Force kill 5 civilians in a gun and bomb attack at the Chlorane Bar, North Ireland
Teton Dam in Idaho bursts, causing $1 billion in damage and resulting in 14 deaths
31st Tony Awards: "The Shadow Box" (play) & "Annie" (musical) win
Coup in Seychelles (National Day)
Seychelles adopts constitution
Soyuz T-2 carries 2 cosmonauts to Salyut 6 space station
AIDS epidemic officially begins when US Centers for Disease Control reports on pneumonia affecting five homosexual men in Los Angeles
World's first today in history program with editable data "TODAY", invented by Michael Butler runs for the first time on a mainframe computer
"Murphy's Law" by Cheri hits #39
Waterfront streetcar begins operating in Seattle
37th Tony Awards: "Torch Song Trilogy" (play) and "Cats" (musical) win
Steve Cauthen wins aboard Slip Anchor at Epsom Downs to become the only jockey to win both the Kentucky Derby (1978) and The Derby
"Nightline" presents its first "Town Meeting," the subject is AIDS and the show runs until 3:47 AM
1st Children's Miracle Network Telethon raises $590,000
Kay Cottee sails into Sydney as the first woman to circle the globe alone
Longest champagne cork flight is 177 feet 9 inches in New York
Russian Orthodox Church celebrates its 1,000th anniversary
Toronto Blue Jays Skydome stadium opens, Milwaukee Brewers win 5-3
Lesbian priest Elizabeth Carl ordained in Episcopal Church, Washington, D.C.
Space Shuttle STS 40 (Columbia 12) launched
"Livin' On The Edge" by Aerosmith hits #18
Somali warlord Mohamed Farrah murders 23 UN peacekeepers from Pakistan
7th Children's Miracle Network Telethon
The Bose-Einstein condensate is first created
Howard Stern Radio Show premieres in Memphis, Tennessee, on WMFS 92.9 FM
A strike begins at the General Motors parts factory in Flint, Michigan, that quickly spreads to five other assembly plants (the strike lasted seven weeks)
Armed conflict between Rwanda and Uganda erupts in Kinsangani, a city in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
OPEC ministers agree to leave the cartel's oil production quotas unchanged for at least a month, until a scheduled emergency meeting July 3

Tropical Storm Allison makes landfall on the upper Texas coastline as a strong tropical storm and dumps large amounts of rain over Houston, causing $5.5 billion in damages, the then costliest tropical storm in US history
U.S. Senator Jim Jeffords leaves the Republican Party, an act which shifts control of the United States Senate from the Republicans to the Democratic Party
A severe heat wave across Pakistan and India reaches its peak, as temperatures exceed 50°C (122°F) in the region
59th Tony Awards: "Monty Python's Spamalot" (musical) & "Doubt: A Parable" (play) win
Serbia declares independence from the State Union of Serbia and Montenegro
MESSENGER spacecraft performs a second flyby of Venus
Paul McCartneyreleases "Memory Almost Full", in US; his fourteenth solo studio album was the first release on Starbucks' Hear Music label
At least 32 people are killed in Peru during clashes between the police and indigenous protesters
Chile officially enters recession; it is the first South American country to enter the global recession
American gubernatorial recall election is held in Wisconsin. Governor Scott Walker wins and becomes the first governor to survive a recall election.
44 people are killed by a lightning storm in Bihar, India
MLB Draft: Cathedral Catholic HS (CA) pitcher Brady Aiken first pick by Houston Astros
6.0 magnitude earthquake (strongest to affect Malaysia since 1978) strikes Ranau, Sabah, Malaysia, killing 18 people on Mount Kinabalu
Swiss vote to reject referendum to give each citizen a guaranteed income of $2,500 Swiss francs per month
Bahrain, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Egypt sever ties with Qatar, citing its support of terrorist groups, Yemen, the Maldives and Libya follow suit
Bus crashes with a truck in Bareilly, Uttar Pradesh, India, 22 die in resulting fire
Montenegro becomes the 29th member of NATO
Puerto Rico declares its Zika virus epidemic over
US Judge Aaron Persky, criticized for his leniency towards campus sex attacker Brock Turner, becomes the 1st judge voted out in 80 years in Santa Clara, California
Denmark general election won by Mette Frederiksen's Social Democrats, defeating sitting Prime Minister Lars Lokke Rasmussen's Liberal party
Ohio doctor William Husel charged with 25 counts of murder for prescribing potentially fatal doses of opioids in Franklin County
On average, people ingest 50,000 pieces of microplastic a year and breathe in similar amount, according to first-ever such study published in journal "Environmental Science and Technology"
At least 160 killed by suspected Islamist extremists in Solhan, Burkina Faso amid a deepening security crisis in the region
At least 50 Nigerian church worshippers, including children, killed by gunman armed with explosives at St Francis Catholic Church, Owo, south-western Nigeria
Australian woman Kathleen Folbigg is pardoned after 20 years in prison for the murder of her four children, after new genetic research found evidence of gene disorders in the children
In western Haiti heavy rain and flooding leaves at least 42 people dead and thousands displaced
Boeing Starliner's Crew Flight Test mission launches aboard an Atlas V rocket from Cape Canaveral with NASA astronauts Butch Wilmore and Suni Williams
Sudanese paramilitary group RSF attacks village of Wad al-Noura in central state of Gezira, killing at least 100 people, forcibly displacing the population
World's only surviving wild horse, the Przewalski's Horse, is returned to the Golden Steppe of Kazakhstan for the first time in over 200 years
Titusand his Roman legions breach the middle wall of Jerusalem
Frederick Henry, Prince of Orange conquers Roermond
Isaac Newtonadmitted as a student to Trinity College, Cambridge
NaturalistsAlexander von HumboldtandAimé Bonplandset sail in the Pizarro from A Coruña, Spain, beginning their 5 year expedition to Latin American
Future 1st computer programmerAda Lovelacemeets mathematician and inventorCharles Babbage
Statue of princeWilliam the Silent, Prince of Orange by Lodewyk Royer unveiled in The Hague's Het Plein
William Tecumseh Shermanrefuses the Republican presidential nomination, saying, "I will not accept if nominated and will not serve if elected."
US Democrats nominateGrover Clevelandfor president
EmperorWilhelm IIresponds to growing demands from Polish and other Slavic peoples living within German territory by calling for more 'Germanization' of the Slavs
First rivet driven on Bank of Italy headquarters at 1 Powell in San Francisco (laterBank of America)
Henry Fordinitiates a 32 hour work week
GeneralCharles De Gaullebecomes French junior minister of Defense
Sandor Szabo beatsB Nagurskiin St Louis, to become wrestling champ
German Field MarshalErwin Rommelgoes on leave just before WWII D-Day landings by the Allies
Benjamin Britten's opera "Peter Grimes" premieres in London
US Secretary of State George Marshall outlines the "Marshall Plan" to rebuild Western Europe
PhilliesRichie Ashburnsets NL rookie consecutive hitting streak at 23
Enid Blyton's wooden toy character Noddy first appears in the "Sunday Graphic"
First nationally televised sporting event,Jersey Joe WalcottdefeatsEzzard Charlesin 15 rounds for the heavyweight boxing title at Municipal Stadium in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Ecclesiae fastos
NY YankeeMickey Mantlehits 550' HR off Chicago Billy Pierce
"Milton BerleShow" last airs on NBC-TV
The first independent government of the State of Singapore is sworn in withLee Kuan Yewas Prime Minister
A state of siege is declared in Iran, andAyatollah Khomeiniis arrested
Davie Jones & King Bees release debut single "I Can't Help Thinking About Me"; group disbands but Davie Jones goes on to success asDavid Bowie
Yugoslav president JosipTitovisits USSR
As'Reggie Jacksonand Bill North engage in clubhouse fight at Detroit
Egyptian presidentAnwar Sadatreopens Suez Canal (closed since 1967)
Los Angeles Dodgers retire future Baseball Hall of Fame managerWalter Alston's #24
National Council of Women of Kenya led byWangari Maathaimarch to Kamukunji Park, Nairobi, and plant seven trees - beginning of the Green Belt Movement
George Harrisonreleases "Somewhere in England", his ninth studio album, includesJohn Lennontribute "All Those Years Ago" (Featuring Paul and Ringo)
MLB Houston Astros pitcherNolan RyanpassesEarly Wynnas all-time walk leader (1,777)
Indira Gandhiorders an attack on Sikhism's holiest site, the Golden Temple in Amritsar
San Diego PadreSteve Garveyejected for 1st time in his 16 year MLB career for arguing about Atlanta's triple play
Dwight Goodenreturns from drug rehabilitation and wins the game
Paul McCartneyreleases "Flowers in the Dirt", his eighth solo studio album, featuring theElvis Costellocollaboration "My Brave Face"
South African troops plunderNelson Mandela's home
Mikhail Gorbachevreceives his 1990 Nobel Peace Prize
Liberian Charles Taylors rebellion kills 550 fugitives
Spalding Gray's one-man show "Gray's Anatomy" opens an encore run at Beaumont Theater, NYC; runs for 8 performances
"The Truman Show" is released starringJim Carrey, Laura Linney, andEd Harris
American actressChloë Grace Moretzwins three MTV Movie & TV Awards: Best Breakthrough Performance, Best Hero, and Biggest Badass Star
Nawaz Sharifis sworn in as Prime Minister of Pakistan
The first article based on NSA leaked documents byEdward Snowdenare published by the Guardian Newspaper in the UK
Alex Pucciosustains a knee injury during the IFSC World Cup competition in Vail, Colorado, requiring surgery
American boxerFloyd Mayweather Jr.named 2017 top earning sportsperson by Forbes with $285 million
Film producerHarvey Weinsteinpleads not guilty to rape and sexual assault charges in court in New York City
US PresidentDonald Trumpadministration's policy of separating immigrant children from their families violates international law according to the UN
Turkish President Recep TayyipErdoganpromises to save the country's shores from a build-up of 'sea snot' (marine mucilage) due to pollution and climate change
American rockerBruce Springsteenjoins British rock bandColdplayfor two songs (The Land of Hope and Dreams; Dancing in the Dark) at Met Life Stadium in East Rutherford, New Jersey
Former US Vice PresidentMike Pencedeclares his candidacy for the Republican presidential nomination
Charles of Salerno is captured by Roger of Lauria during a naval battle in the Gulf of Naples, part of the Sicilian Vespers uprising
Anti-slavery novel "Uncle Tom's Cabin" byHarriet Beecher Stowefirst published in serial form in "The National Era"
After receiving favorable weather reports, GeneralEisenhowerdecides to proceed with the D-Day invasion on June 6
Six-Day War begins between Israel and the neighboring Arab states of Egypt, Jordan and Syria
Palestinian Sirhan Sirhan assassinatesRobert F. Kennedy, shooting him 3 times and wounding 5 others at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles, California. Kennedy dies the next day.
Edmund of Langley, 1st Duke of York, son of Edward III of England, born in Kings Langley, Hertfordshire, England (d. 1402)
Justus Jonas, German Protestant theologian and reformer, born in Nordhausen, Thuringia, Germany (d. 1555)
Margaret of France, Duchess of Berry, born in Château de Saint-Germain-en-Laye, France (d. 1573)
Bernardino Baldi, Italian mathematician and writer, born in Urbino, Marche, Italy (d. 1617)
Pu Songling, Qing Dynasty Chinese writer (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio), born in Zibo, Shandong (d. 1715)
Joseph Pitton de Tournefort, French botanist, born in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1708)
Sarah Churchill, Duchess of Marlborough, close friend of Queen Anne of Great Britain, born in Hertfordshire (d. 1744)
Nicolas Bernier, French composer, born in Mantes-sur-Seine, France (d. 1734)
Cristoph Raupach, Danish composer, born in Tønder, Denmark (d. 1758)

Willem Frederik van Bylandt, Dutch military officer, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1855)

George Thorndike Angell, American lawyer and advocate for the humane treatment of animals (ASPCA), born in Southbridge, Massachusetts (d. 1909)
Jabez Lamar Monroe Curry, American politician (Rep-Ala, 1857-61), born in Lincoln County, Georgia (d. 1903)
Ivar Hallström, Swedish composer, born in Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1901)
Beverly Holcombe Robertson, Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Amelia County, Virginia (d. 1910)
Marcus Joseph Wright, American lawyer and Brigadier General (Confederate Army), born in Purdy, Tennessee (d. 1922)
Marie of the Netherlands, Princess of Orange Nassau, born in Wassenaar, Netherlands (d. 1910)
Samuel Garman, American zoologist, ichthyologist, herpetologist, and namesake of a Jamaican lizard, born in Indiana County, Pennsylvania (d. 1927)
Pat Garrett, American Western lawman, born in Chambers County, Alabama (d. 1908)
William E. Upjohn, American physician who invented dissolvable pills (Upjohn Pharmaceutical Company), born in Richland, Michigan (d. 1932)[1]
Allvar Gullstrand, Swedish physician (Nobel 1911), born in Landskrona, Sweden (d. 1930)
Arthur Somervell, English composer, born in Windermere, Westmorland (d. 1937)

Johan Willem Albarda, 1st Dutch socialist minister (1939-45), born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1957)
William Franklin "Frank" Smith, American actor (Cowboy & Bandit, Scarlet Car, Frontier Days), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1961)
Adolf Wiklund, Swedish composer, born in Långserud, Värmland, Sweden (d. 1950)
René Pottier, French racing cyclist (Tour de France 1906), born in Moret-sur-Loing, Seine-et-Marne, France (d. 1907)
Robert Mayer, German-born businessman and philanthropist, born in Mannheim, German Empire (d. 1985)
Claude Jennings, Australian cricket batsman (6 Tests, HS 32; South Australia, Queensland), born in Melbourne, Australia (d. 1950)
Ivy Compton-Burnett, English novelist (Manservant and Maidservant), born in Pinner, Middlesex, England (d. 1969)
Ralph Benatzky, Czech composer, born in Moravské Budějovice, Czech Republic (d. 1957)

Ruth Benedict, American anthropologist (Patterns of Culture), born in New York City (d. 1948)
Raden Mas Noto Soeroto, Javanese poet (Melatiknoppen), born in Yogyakarta, Indonesia (d. 1951)

August Baeyens, Flemish composer (Coriolanus), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1966)

Salvatore Ferragamo, Italian luxury shoe designer (Salvatore Ferragamo S.p.A.), born in Bonito, Italy (d. 1960)

John Abbott, British actor (The Jungle Book, Gigi, Smogasboard), born in London, England (d. 1996)
John Kemp, 1st Viscount Rochdale, British soldier and businessman, born in England (d. 1993)
Margaret Rawlings, British actress (Roman Holiday), born in Osaka, Japan (d. 1996)
Rolf Bongs, German writer, born in Dusseldolf (d. 1981)

Dean Amadon, American ornithologist, born in Milwaukee, Wisconsin (d. 2003)
Eric Hollies, English cricket spin bowler (13 Tests, 44 wickets; dismissed Donald Bradman for 0 in his final Test innings), born in Old Hill, Staffordshire, England (d. 1981)
Josef Neckermann, German equestrian rider (Olympic gold dressage 1964, 68), born in Würzburg, Germany (d. 1992)
Friedrich Wildgans, Austrian composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1965)

Lancelot Ware, English barrister and co-founder of Mensa, the international society for intellectually gifted people, born in Mitcham, Surrey, England (d. 2000)
Eddie Joost, American baseball shortstop (MLB All Star 1949, 52; World Series 1940 Cincinnati Reds; Philadelphia Athletics) and manager (Philadelphia A's), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2011)
Jack Sutherland, Scottish journalist, born in Aberdeen, Scotland (d. 1996)
Sid Barnes, Australian cricket batsmen (13 Tests, 3 x 100, 5 x 50, HS 234; NSWCA), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1973)
Branimir Sakač, Croatian composer (Matrix Symphony), born in Zagreb (d. 1979)
Akeo Watanabe, Japanese conductor (Japan Philharmonic, 1956-68 & 1978-83), born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 1990)

Cornelius Ryan, Irish war reporter and historian (Bridge too Far), born in Dublin, Ireland (d. 1974)
Kurt Edelhagen, German jazz pianist and big band bandleader, born in Herne, Germany (d. 1982)
Marion Motley, American NFL fullback (all-time leading AAFC rusher), born in Leesburg, Georgia (d. 1999)
Lancelot Pierre, West Indian cricketer (1 Test WI v England 1948, 0-28), born in Port of Spain, Trinidad (d. 1989)
Gordon "Specs" Powell, American jazz and studio drummer and percussionist (CBS Orchestra, 1943-72), born in New York City (d. 2007)
Daniel Pinkham, American organist, composer (Signs of the Zodiac) and educator (New England Conservatory), born in Lynn, Massachusetts (d. 2006)
Art Donovan, American NFL HOF defensive tackle, 1950-61 (4 x Pro Bowl, 2 x Champion - Baltimore Colts and 2 other teams), and US Marine (WWII - Iwo Jima), born in Bronx, New York (d. 2013)
Bill Hayes, American singer (Your Show of Shows; The Ballad of Davy Crockett"), and actor (Days of Our Lives. 1970-2023 - "Doug"), born in Harvey, Illinois (d. 2024)
Dorothy Claire [Marietta Wright], American big band singer (Bobby Byrne; Winchell & Mahoney Show), born in Laporte, Indiana (d. 1982)
William L. Dickinson, American politician (Rep-R-AL, 1965-93), born in Opelika, Alabama (d. 2008)
Don Chargin, American boxing promoter (IBHOF; Bobby Chacon, Tony "The Tiger" Lopez, Loreto Garza), born in Santa Clara, California (d. 2018)

Nikolay Nikolayevich Sidel'nikov, Russian composer, born in Tver, Soviet Union (d. 1992)

Jerzy Prokopiuk, Polish philosopher and anthroposopist, born in Warsaw (d. 2021)

Curt Michel, American astrophysicist and NASA astronaut, born in LaCrosse Wisconsin (d. 2015)
Vilhjálmur Einarsson, Icelandic athlete (Olympic silver triple-jump 1956; Iceland's first ever Olympic medal), born in Reyðarfjörður, Iceland (d. 2019)

Floyd Butler, American soul singer (Friends of Distinction - "Grazing In The Grass"), born in San Diego, California (d. 1990)
Allan Ahlberg, British children's author with his wife Janet (The Jolly Postman, Each Peach Pear Plum, Funnybones), born in Croydon, England (d. 2025)
Jim Murray, American football executive (GM Philadelphia Eagles 1973-82) and co-founder of the Ronald McDonald House, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2025)
Karin Balzer, German athlete (Olympic gold 80m hurdles 1964; bronze 100m hurdles 1972), born in Magdeburg, Germany (d. 2019)
